title: Deferred Tax Accounting
description:
Premium Analysis Guide: AS vs Ind AS 12 vs IFRS 12 vs US GAAP ASC
            740

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION A: Core Technical Comparison Table
tableData: Income statement approach; deferred tax is an adjunct to tax expense.
Balance sheet approach; deferred tax = temporary differences between carrying amount and tax base.
Same balance sheet approach as Ind AS.
Same balance sheet approach, but with specific exceptions (e.g., outside basis differences).
From timing of expense recognition to full measurement of future tax consequences of assets/liabilities.
Recognised only for timing differences that reverse in future periods.
Recognised for all temporary differences (including those from initial recognition exceptions).
Similar, but includes an exception for leveraged leases and certain outside basis differences.
Broader scope – includes more items like revaluations, business combinations, and ROU assets.
Using enacted tax rates (substantively enacted not always clearly defined).
Using enacted or substantively enacted tax rates expected to apply when temporary difference reverses.
Uses enacted tax rates (substantively enacted not permitted).
Ind AS/IFRS allow slightly forward-looking rates; US GAAP is stricter.
Recognised only if virtual certainty of future profits (for carry-forward losses).
Recognised to extent probable that taxable profit available.
Recognised if more likely than not (>50%) that benefit will be realised.
US GAAP threshold is lower (more likely than not) vs Ind AS/IFRS (probable = higher threshold).
Limited re-assessment; not systematically required each period.
Same, with valuation allowance adjustments each period.
Dynamic adjustment reflects changing profit forecasts.
Effect recognised in P&L in period of change.
Effect recognised in P&L or OCI depending on where original item was recognised.
More precise matching of tax effect to underlying transaction.
Deferred tax on asset/liability differences recognised but not fully integrated.
Recognise deferred tax on all temporary differences in acquisition accounting; affects goodwill.
Similar, with additional detailed guidance on tax-deductible goodwill.
Acquisition accounting now reflects true tax position of acquired entity.
Current/non-current classification based on nature of timing difference.
Always non-current; no offset between different tax jurisdictions.
Can offset current and non-current if legally enforceable right and same jurisdiction.
Ind AS/IFRS more conservative (no netting across types).
Allocated to P&L only; no allocation to OCI or equity.
Allocated to P&L, OCI, or equity based on where original transaction was recognised.
Tax effect follows the item – critical for comprehensive income analysis.
Basic: breakdown of timing differences and rates.
Extensive: reconciliation of expected vs actual tax rate, unrecognised DTAs, expiry dates, etc.
Even more detailed: valuation allowance movements, uncertain tax positions tabular rollforward.
Ind AS/IFRS and US GAAP provide far greater transparency into tax risk.
No specific guidance; general provision concept.
No specific standard; use IFRIC 23 (recognition based on probable outcome).
Detailed two-step: more likely than not to sustain → measurement at largest benefit >50% likely.
US GAAP most prescriptive; Ind AS/ IFRS more principles-based.
Temporary Difference Only timing differences Deferred Tax Liability (End Y1) 5.00 Cr P&L Tax Expense Impact 5.00 Cr Note Misses other temp diffs
Temporary Difference All taxable temporary differences Deferred Tax Liability (End Y1) 5.00 Cr P&L Tax Expense Impact 5.00 Cr Note Comprehensive
Temporary Difference All taxable temporary differences Deferred Tax Liability (End Y1) 5.00 Cr P&L Tax Expense Impact 5.00 Cr Note Same as Ind AS

Temporary Difference All taxable temp diffs, plus valuation allowance Deferred Tax Liability (End Y1) 5.00 Cr (assuming no valuation allowance) P&L Tax Expense Impact 5.00 Cr Note Threshold for DTAs stricter

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION F: Balance Sheet Impact - Year 1
tableData: Similar but valuation allowance reduces DTA
5.00 Cr added back in operating CF
Same here but diverges with uncertain positions
AS may overstate ROE if DTL not recognized properly
AS understates leverage due to lack of deferred liabilities

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION I: Earnings Quality - Signals Table
tableData: Large DTA with no valuation allowance
Management highly confident of future profits
Could be optimistic; check historical forecast accuracy.
Stress-test future profitability; compare to industry.
Prior DTA no longer considered realisable
Past earnings may have been overstated; negative signal.
Reduce reliance on deferred tax assets in valuation.
May be sustainable (e.g., tax incentives) or low-quality (one-off).
Identify drivers; normalise ETR for forecasting.
Deferred tax liability shrinking despite growth
Reversing temp differences faster than new ones created
Business may be slowing capex; watch for under-investment.
Compare capex trend with DTL movement.
No reconciliation of expected vs actual tax rate
Request full tax note; treat as amber flag.
Normalise by removing one-off tax settlements or rate changes.
Exclude unrecognised DTA benefit from adjusted earnings.
Add back DTL to equity for return calc.
Focus on cash tax paid for liquidity.
Identify drivers: permanent differences, DTA realisation, or one-offs.
May be lower quality if driven by aggressive DTA recognition.
Compare to capex intensity and tax depreciation rates.
High DTL often means accelerated tax benefits – positive for cash flow.
High ratio suggests management lacks confidence in future profits.
Low ratio indicates large timing differences (favourable).
Sustainable cash advantage if timing differences are long-dated.
Is the low ETR real earnings quality?
Risk of one-off or aggressive DTA.
Not always; check reconciliation of statutory to actual rate.
Permanent differences (e.g., exempt income) are high quality.
Why is DTA growing but no valuation allowance?
Requires judgement; compare forecast accuracy over 3-5 years.
Do I treat DTL as debt?
No – DTL is future tax, not contractual obligation to lender.
But DTL reduces free cash flow when it reverses.
How do I compare across standards?
AS vs Ind AS vs US GAAP.
Reconstruct DTA/DTL using balance sheet approach for all.
Ignore AS presentation; normalise to Ind AS/IFRS.
DTA is worthless without profits to utilise it.
Normalise ETR to statutory rate for valuation.
Prefer stable cash tax over volatile deferred tax.
Exclude DTL from debt; focus on cash tax payable.
See tax asset as latent value.
Model probability of utilisation before ascribing value.
May be sustainable if truly permanent (e.g., tax holidays).
Verify expiry date of tax incentives.
DTA may be at risk; management hiding impairment.
Request full rollforward of valuation allowance.
Provides detailed tax reconciliation and uncertain position table
High-quality tax governance; management not hiding risk.
Green flag; rely more on reported ETR.
Suddenly reverses large DTA without explanation
Recast prior year earnings; treat as red flag.

sections.sectionTitle: SECTION P: Red Flags vs Green Flags (Visual Indicator Matrix)
tableData: Large DTA with no valuation allowance despite recent losses
Demand to see the precise forecast showing probable recovery.
Write down core earnings metrics; management is hiding deterioration.
No disclosure of uncertain tax positions in cross-border firms
Assume maximum liability scenario for valuation.
ETR below statutory rate for >3 years with no permanent benefit explanation
Normalize ETR to statutory rate for all DCF projections.
DTA with reasonable valuation allowance linked to clear forecast
Clear waterfall of uncertain tax positions (FIN 48 equivalent)
Use provided probabilities directly in DCF risk-weighting.
